title: practical english usage - Part 128
content:
Either is pronounced /'aɪðə(r)/ or /'i:ðə(r)/ (in American English usually /'i:ðər/).

There are roses on either side of the door.

In these expressions, either sometimes means 'each'.

5 either       side/end If either of the boys phones, tell him/them I'll be in this evening.

informal).

pronoun, the later pronoun can be singular (more formal) or plural (more When a pronoun is used later in a clause to refer back to either + noun/ 4 pronouns 'Wouldyou like tea or coffee?' 7 don't mind. Either.' We   can use either alone if the meaning is clear.

3 without      a  noun She Just doesn't care what either of her parents say(s).

Either of the children is perfectly capable of looking after the baby.

informal style.

A  verb after either of is more often singular, but it can sometimes be plural in an I don't like either of them.

I don't like either of my history teachers.

You can use either of the bathrooms.

A  following noun is plural.

We use either of before a determiner (for example the, my, these) or a pronoun.

She didn't get on with either parent, (     .. .-eitke^parents) not Come on Tuesday or Thursday. Either day is OK. (     Either days...) We   use either with a singular noun to mean 'one or the other' of two.

1  either       + singular 156  either not Neither of them is here, (     Betk-of-tkem-aremet-here^ Instead of both ... not, we  normally use neither (►   157).

6 negative       structures Both of us can swim.  Both (of) the children have gone to bed.

(see above).

Note that these meanings can also be expressed by using both (of) with a subject grammar «158    some generally used. For details, ►     161.

questions which  expect or encourage the answer ’ Yes’ . In other cases, any is With this meaning, some is most common in affirmative clauses, and in 3 some      and   any For more about ’ strong* and ’ weak’  pronunciations, ►    3  i 5.

some /s(əjm/ new clothes /s(ə)m/ before (adjective +) noun.

When  some has this indefinite meaning, it usually has a ’ weak’  pronunciation 2 pronunciation Would you like some tea?

I need some new clothes.

used when it is not important to say exactly how  much/many we  are thinking of.

Some is a determiner. It often suggests an indefinite quantity or number, and is 1  meaning:      indefinite       quantity/number 158  some For neither. .. nor, ►    228.

In American English, the usual pronunciation is /'nɪ:ðər/.

In British English, neither can be pronounced both /'naiðə(r)/ and /' ni:ðə(r)/.

'Which one do you want?1 'Neither.' We   can use neither without a noun or pronoun, if the meaning is clear.

3 neither        used    alone Neither of my sisters are married.

In an informal style, a plural verb is possible.

Neither of my sisters is married.

After neither of + noun/pronoun, we  use a singular verb in a formal style.

Neither of us saw it happen.

Neither of my brothers can sing, (     Neither-my brothers ean sing.) pronoun. The  noun or pronoun is plural.

We   use neither of before a determiner (for example the, my, these), and before a 2  neither        of   + plural 'Can you come on Monday or Tuesday?' 'I'm afraid neither day is possible.' two)’ .

We   use neither before a singular noun to mean ’ not one and not the other (of 1 neither        + singular      noun 157 neithe      r Section13 Determiners:       Quantifiers grammar *158  some something, or that we  do not think much of him/her/it.

We can use this structure to suggest that we  are not interested in somebody or She's living in some village in Yorkshire.

There must be some job I could do.

Some idiot has taken the bath plug.

countable noun).

Some (/sʌm/) can refer to an unknown person or thing (usually with a singular 8  an   unknown       person     or  thing I've got some money, but not enough.

Some of us were late, but we were all there by ten o'clock.

Some people like the sea; others prefer the mountains.

others, all or enough.

Some (pronounced /sʌm/)  can have a more emphatic meaning, contrasting with 7 contrast      with    others,       etc some /sʌm/   of us   Would you like some /sʌm/^ Before of, or with no following noun, some is pronounced /sʌm/.
